articles.title: The social contract of writing
articles.summary: LLMs are increasingly making inroads into writing, with many people relying on these artificial intelligence tools for assistance and even entire content creation. However this reliance is not only a sign of laziness but also undermines the social contract between writers and readers, where it's assumed that writers have put in more intellectual effort to produce high-quality work. The use of LLMs can lead to a homogenization of writing styles and ideas, with authors often relying on these tools to generate content rather than putting in the time and effort required to write it themselves.

articles.title: Nix's Substituter List Is Not a Routing Table
articles.summary: Nix's substituter model is a design that combines binary caches for speed and locality, but its simplicity can lead to issues when dealing with large configurations or multiple dependencies. The key technology behind this approach is the use of a static integer priority field in nix.conf, which determines the order in which substituters are consulted. However, this prioritization is based on static information that does not account for factors like latency and cache proximity. As a result, the daemon's network layer can become inefficient when dealing with complex paths or large multi-cache setups.

articles.title: Discussion about C array type semantics
articles.summary: C array types are distinct from pointer types in terms of how they're used and interpreted. An array type T[n] represents a contiguous sequence of T values, but you can't directly refer to these values. Instead, any expression that would be of this type is converted to a pointer type T*. This conversion happens when the array indexing operator arr[ix] operates on pointers, allowing for treating arrays like pointers. In contrast, functions are treated differently and don't decay to their first argument when passed as an array or function value.
